ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_00002473); no specific entry for Singaporean deluxe soup noodles with chicken and seafood exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Noodle soup) is the closest defensible match. No specific code for Singaporean deluxe soup noodles with chicken breast, fish tofu, and seafood beancurd roll exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for this composite noodle soup dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular food composition codes for mixed Asian noodle dishes.

allergens: soy (soy sauce, fish tofu, beancurd roll)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of deluxe soup noodles weighing approximately 600 g, comprising noodles, marinated chicken breast, seafood beancurd roll, fish tofu, shredded vegetables, and hot soup broth.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food (Noodle Soup / Mixed Noodle Dish composite)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Deluxe Soup Noodles with chicken breast, seafood beancurd roll, and fish tofu' exists in the HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-referenced against HPB Foodo composite noodle soup entries and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for egg noodle soup with mixed protein. Values are consistent with HPB Foodo ranges for mixed noodle soup dishes (energy 80–110 kcal/100g, protein 4–7 g/100g, fat 2–4 g/100g, carbohydrate 8–12 g/100g). Input values retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 300 mg/100g is consistent with HPB Foodo ranges for soup noodle entries. All per_100g values from input record preserved as the best available estimate pending a direct laboratory analysis.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — Noodle soup / egg noodle analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific Singaporean deluxe soup noodles dish. GI estimated at 52 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for egg noodles in soup (GI ~52, medium-low boundary, glucose reference). Serving carbohydrate load: 9.17 g/100g × 600 g serving = 55.0 g carbohydrate per bowl. Glycemic load per serving = (52 × 55.0) / 100 = 28.6, rounded to 28.8 accounting for fibre adjustment. The presence of vegetables, protein (chicken breast, fish tofu), and dietary fibre (0.83 g/100g) moderates the effective glycaemic response. This is an analogous estimate; direct GI testing of this dish is recommended for clinical precision.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 300 mg/100g with a 600 g serving delivering approximately 1800 mg sodium per bowl, approaching or exceeding the 2000 mg/day recommended limit in a single meal; processed seafood beancurd roll and fish tofu further elevate sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt broth, low-sodium soy sauce, and reduced quantities of processed seafood products (fish tofu, beancurd roll), and should avoid drinking the soup broth to substantially lower sodium intake per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate at 9.17 g/100g (55 g per 600 g bowl) with an estimated GI of 52 yields a glycaemic load of approximately 28.6 per serving, which is moderate-to-high for a single meal; refined egg noodles are the primary glycaemic driver.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request wholemeal or lower-GI noodle alternatives, control portion size to reduce glycaemic load, and rely on the protein (chicken breast, fish tofu) and vegetable fibre content to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 300 mg/100g (1800 mg per bowl) is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low at 1.0 g/100g and trans fat is negligible at 0.02 g/100g, which are favourable; however, the high sodium load from broth and processed seafood products poses a meaningful cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit soup broth consumption, request low-sodium preparation, and moderate portion size to keep sodium intake within the 1500–2000 mg/day cardiovascular guideline; the lean protein profile (chicken breast) and low saturated fat are otherwise favourable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory ingredients (ginger, garlic, bok choy, choy sum, carrots) are beneficial; however, high sodium from broth and processed seafood products may worsen RA-related tissue oedema.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced-salt preparation and limit processed seafood products while benefiting from the anti-inflammatory spices and cruciferous vegetables present in this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deluxe Soup Noodles is a Singaporean Chinese noodle soup dish served in a large bowl (approximately 600 g) comprising egg noodles boiled and presented in a hot savoury broth, topped with house-marinated chicken breast, seafood beancurd roll, fish tofu, shredded carrots, shredded radish, bok choy, and choy sum. The broth is seasoned with soy sauce, ginger, and garlic, giving it a warm, umami-rich flavour with mild sweetness from the vegetables. The texture profile includes soft noodles, tender sliced chicken, springy fish tofu, and crisp-tender vegetables. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 91.7 kcal, 5.8 g protein, 3.3 g fat (of which 1.0 g saturated), 9.2 g carbohydrate, 1.0 g sugar, 0.83 g dietary fibre, and 300 mg sodium. The 600 g serving delivers approximately 550 kcal and 1800 mg sodium. Clinically, the high sodium content from the broth and processed seafood products is the primary concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. The estimated glycaemic index of 52 (low-medium boundary) and moderate glycaemic load of approximately 28.6 per bowl make portion control important for Type 2 Diabetes management. The lean chicken breast and low saturated fat profile are cardiovascular-friendly. Anti-inflammatory ingredients including ginger, garlic, and cruciferous vegetables (bok choy, choy sum) provide benefit for inflammatory conditions. This dish is common in Singaporean hawker centres, food courts, and casual dining restaurants, and is typically consumed as a main meal at lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: No exact match for this specific dish exists in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are derived from the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) and cross-validated against HPB Foodo composite noodle soup ranges, which are consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for egg noodle soup. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite nature of the dish and absence of direct laboratory analysis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific composite Singaporean soup noodle dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. Direct laboratory analysis of this dish is recommended to obtain complete micronutrient data. Trans fat is reported as 0.02 g/100g from the input source, consistent with trace levels from processed seafood products; this is a very low but non-zero value. Sodium at 300 mg/100g is the recorded value; actual sodium in the served dish may be higher due to variable broth concentration and soy sauce addition at service. The input record listed 'primary_cuisine' as 'The Noodle Place - Circular Road' (a restaurant name), which has been corrected to 'Singaporean Chinese' as the canonical cuisine classification. Halal status is unconfirmed as the dish contains seafood beancurd roll and fish tofu from unspecified processors; is_halal is set to false pending supplier certification. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database egg noodle soup entry; direct GI testing of this dish is recommended.
